title: Bourse has best day in six weeks
full_text:
Higher-than-expected unemployment data and a strong lead from Wall Street pushed the Australian sharemarket to its best day for six weeks.
The unemployment rate rose 0.2 points to 4.5 per cent in April. Encouraged by the strong sign interest rates will be held next month, the S&P/ASX 200 rose 1.47 per cent to 8621.7, the largest single-day gain since April 8.
The All Ords was up 1.4 per cent, and eight of 11 sectors finished in the green. IG analyst Tony Sycamore said rising unemployment was good news for the local sharemarket with its heavy weighting toward interest-rate sensitive financial, real estate and consumer discretionary stocks.
“The April employment report showed a fall of 18,600 jobs, well below the expected gain of 15,000,” he said.
“While a slowing jobs market is unwelcome news for job hunters, it is good news for the ASX 200. This is because it reduces the likelihood of further aggressive RBA rate hikes this year.” Wall Street snapped a three-day losing streak overnight on Wednesday, and the jobs data pushed the Australian dollar down.
Australia’s mining equities led the way on Thursday, with a sector gain of 2.6 per cent. Market heavyweight BHP rallied 3.1 per cent to $59.10 and Rio Tinto stormed 3.2 per cent ahead to $181.68.
Evolution Mining also lifted 3.8 per cent to $11.80, but fellow gold miner Northern Star fell 2.1 per cent to $18.94 as its managing director of 13 years announced he was stepping down.
Battery minerals producer and explorer Liontown spiked 4.2 per cent to $2.25. James Hardie jumped 5.4 per cent as Macquarie and Morgans analysts lowered price targets but kept them above the current $27.99 price; down to $39.60 and $39 respectively.
Jewellery retailer and piercer SkinKandy made its ASX debut, rising 6.8 per cent to $2.35.
Stake analyst Samy Sriram said the float was notable as a divergence from Australian mining and materials IPOs.
“With the recent public market listings of Koala and now SkinKandy, we might be seeing more diversified players make their ASX debut,” she said. “The offering to investors is essentially a retail growth option … the 27 per cent EBITDA margin is impressive for a services-led retailer, and if that holds as the business scales internationally, the valuation becomes easier to justify.” Real estate was also in green territory on the back of the jobs data, with retirement village provider Gemlife Communities rallying 4 per cent to $4.70, Lifestyle Communities enjoyed a 2.7 per cent bump to $4.90.
Goodman, Vicinity Centres, Stockland, Charter Hall and Mirvac all gained over 2 per cent each.
Zip Co rose 2.7 per cent to $2.26 on news it secured the right to use the Zip brand after a legal stoush.
Guzman y Gomez surged 13 per cent to $18.08, partially on the upbeat assessment by RBC Capital Markets analysts.
Banks were also strong, led by NAB, up 2.3 per cent to $37.66. Shares in Australian Agricultural Co firmed 1.2 per cent to $1.31 after the nation’s largest beef and cattle producer reported a 23 per cent jump in full-year operating profit to $71.6m, while revenue increased 9 per cent to $422.1m Telstra shares fell 0.6 per cent to $5.46.
author: BLAIR JACKSON, EQUITIES
heading: Banking and Economy
